title: Open Strings
description: The six open strings — E, A, D, G, B, E (low to high). This is the anchor from which every fretboard landmark is measured. Know them cold and know how they tune relative to each other.
practice: Name all six strings from memory, low to high and high to low.
Use the mnemonic 'Eddie Ate Dynamite, Good Bye Eddie' until it's automatic.
Pluck each string and listen for the pitch difference between adjacent strings.

title: The Fret as a Half Step
description: Each fret raises the pitch of a string by exactly one half step. Twelve frets = one octave. This is the entire logic of fret numbering — everything else follows from it.
practice: Starting on the open A string, name every note up to the 12th fret.
Pick a fret at random — name the note on strings E, A, and D at that fret.
Find three different places on the neck where you can play the note C.

title: Low E String — All 12 Notes
description: The low E string contains all 12 chromatic notes within the first 12 frets. Mastering this single string gives you a mental map for the entire neck — every other string pattern mirrors this one.
practice: Name every note on the low E string from open to the 12th fret without looking at a chart.
Find the note F on the low E string, then find F# and Gb and confirm they're the same fret.
Play the low E string ascending at 60 BPM, saying each note name aloud.

title: Octave Shapes
description: Octave shapes are fixed fingering patterns that locate the same note one octave up, skipping one or two strings. The two most useful shapes: skip one string (2 frets up), skip two strings (same fret or 3 frets up).
practice: Find every G on the neck using only octave shapes starting from the low E string.
Play a slow melody using octave doubles — same melody note played in two octaves simultaneously.
Start on any note and chain three octave shapes to find that note in three regions of the neck.

title: The CAGED System — Introduction
description: CAGED breaks the fretboard into five overlapping chord shapes (C, A, G, E, D), each of which maps a chord voicing, a scale pattern, and an arpeggio pattern to the same region of the neck. Understanding CAGED ties chord and scale knowledge together.
practice: Play a G major chord in all five CAGED positions up the neck.
For the E shape, name which fingers correspond to root, 3rd, and 5th.
Improvise in G major over a backing track, staying within one CAGED shape per 4 bars.

title: All Strings — Natural Notes
description: The natural notes (A through G, no sharps or flats) are the landmarks of the fretboard. Knowing where every natural note falls on every string is the foundation of real-time fretboard navigation.
practice: Play through every natural note on one string per day until all six are memorized.
Find the note E on all six strings without a reference chart.
For any fret 1–12 called out by a partner, name the natural note nearest to it on any string.

title: Position Playing
description: A position is defined by where your index finger sits — in 5th position, your index covers fret 5, your ring covers 7. Learning to stay in a position and shift deliberately is the key to fluid neck navigation.
practice: Play a two-octave C major scale entirely in 2nd position without open strings.
Play the same scale in 5th position.
Shift from 2nd to 5th position mid-scale fluidly — no pause or fumble.
